1: ἄρχων τῶν Ἰουδαίων — archōn tōn Ioudaiōn, "a ruler of the Jews." Archōn designates a member of the Sanhedrin, the supreme Jewish council. Combined with Φαρισαῖος (Pharisaios), both identifiers mark Nicodemus as an insider of the religious establishment — the opposite of the fishermen disciples.
2:
νυκτός (nyktos) — genitive of time, "by night." The Evangelist places this early and does not explain the motive. Whether shame, caution, or a desire for privacy without crowds, the darkness frames a scene that will turn on the contrast between light and dark (John 3:19–21).

Ῥαββί (Rabbi) — "my great one," a title of respect; Nicodemus addresses Jesus as an equal teacher, not yet as a superior. His opening acknowledgment — "we know you are a teacher come from God" — is accurate but incomplete: the Evangelist has already identified Jesus as far more.
3:
ἄνωθεν (anōthen) — the hinge word of the dialogue. The term means both "again" (temporal, a second time) and "from above" (spatial/theological, from heaven). Nicodemus hears the temporal sense in John 3:4; the Evangelist intends the theological sense throughout. The ambiguity is not a translation problem but a literary device the Evangelist uses to expose the gap between what Jesus says and what a listener embedded in the natural realm can hear.

ἰδεῖν τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ θεοῦ — "to see the kingdom of God." The verb is horaō (aorist infinitive), perception and experience rather than mere observation. The kingdom is not yet entered; it is first seen — a claim that will be sharpened in John 3:5.
4:
δεύτερον (deuteron) — "a second time." Nicodemus has taken ἄνωθεν as "again" (temporal) and pushed it to its most literal extreme: physical re-entry into the womb. His question is not ridicule but genuine incomprehension — he is working with only one semantic register of the word Jesus used.

γέρων ὤν (gerōn ōn) — "being old." The present participle concedes his own situation: the natural impossibility is compounded by age. Nicodemus frames the question in terms of biological limits, which is exactly what Jesus's answer will transcend.
5:
ἐξ ὕδατος καὶ πνεύματος — "of water and Spirit." No article before either noun; the Greek pairs them as complementary agents of the new birth, not as two separate acts. The pairing has generated debate: water as physical birth (amniotic fluid), water as baptism, or water as cleansing/purification imagery from Ezekiel 36:25–27, which pairs water-washing with the gift of Spirit. The Ezekiel background is strong given the rebuke in John 3:10 ("you are Israel's teacher").

εἰσελθεῖν εἰς τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ θεοῦ — "to enter the kingdom of God." The step from "see" (John 3:3) to "enter" (John 3:5) is progression: perception precedes participation. The kingdom requires a specific mode of entry.
6:
σάρξ (sárx, G4561, dispute level 4) — "flesh." Here σάρξ designates the natural human realm: all that is generated by human origin and human capacity. It is not yet Paul's term for the sin-power (as in Rom 8); in John's usage it denotes creatureliness and finitude. The chiastic structure is deliberate: "born of flesh / is flesh; born of Spirit / is spirit" — the new birth does not repair the old nature but introduces a categorically different origin.

τὸ γεγεννημένον ἐκ τοῦ πνεύματος — "that which has been born of the Spirit." The perfect passive participle gegenēmenon marks the completed new birth with ongoing effect: the person so born carries that origin permanently.
7:
ὑμᾶς (hymas) — the pronoun shifts to plural: "you [all] must be born again." Jesus has been addressing Nicodemus individually; the plural extends the necessity beyond this one teacher to all. The individual encounter opens into a universal claim.

δεῖ (dei) — "it is necessary." The impersonal verb of divine necessity appears repeatedly in John for what must happen according to God's purpose (cf. John 4:4; John 10:16; John 20:9). The new birth is not optional or aspirational.
8:
τὸ πνεῦμα (to pneuma) — the key wordplay: the same Greek word means both "wind" and "Spirit." Jesus is not using an analogy where wind illustrates Spirit; the same word carries both meanings simultaneously. The verb πνεῖ (pnei, "blows") shares the same root as πνεῦμα — "the spirit/wind spirits/blows where it wills." The invisibility of the wind's origin and destination mirrors the Spirit's freedom and inscrutability.

τὴν φωνὴν αὐτοῦ ἀκούεις — "you hear its/his voice." Phōnē properly means "voice" rather than merely "sound." The sentence works for wind (audible gusting) and for the Spirit (the voice that the born-again person hears). The translation settles for one referent; the Greek holds both.
9: Nicodemus's response is a bare question: Πῶς (Pōs) — "How?" He offers no objection to the substance of what Jesus says, only incomprehension of the mechanism. The question is honest, not combative. Jesus's reply (John 3:10) does not answer the "how" but redirects to the authority behind the teaching.
10: ὁ διδάσκαλος τοῦ Ἰσραήλ — "the teacher of Israel." Both articles are present: not "a teacher" but the teacher, a recognized authoritative figure in the community. The rebuke is gentle but pointed: someone in this role should recognize the categories Jesus is using — they come from Israel's own prophetic texts (Ezek 36; Jer 31).
11: ἡμεῖς / ἡμῶν — "we" / "our." Jesus uses the first-person plural: "we speak of what we know." The shift has puzzled interpreters — does Jesus speak as one voice of a group? Some read the plural as Jesus identifying with the prophetic tradition; others as Jesus speaking as the divine community (Father and Son). The contrast with the plural "you [all]" in the next clause indicates the Evangelist may be addressing the unbelieving audience of his own time through Jesus's words.
12: τὰ ἐπίγεια / τὰ ἐπουράνια — "earthly things" / "heavenly things." The contrast is spatial and also epistemological: earthly things that can be grasped by natural analogy (birth, wind) have already exceeded Nicodemus's comprehension. Heavenly things — the descent of the Son, the giving of the Spirit, the mechanics of new birth from above — will be even harder. Jesus names his own restraint in the teaching.
13:
ἀναβέβηκεν (anabebēken) — perfect tense: "has ascended." The perfect in Greek denotes a past action with a persisting present state. The claim is paradoxical: the one speaking of heavenly things is the one who has "already ascended" — the human perspective on someone who descended from above is that they carry with them the knowledge only ascent could bring.

ὁ ὢν ἐν τῷ οὐρανῷ — "who is/being in heaven." This participial phrase ("the one being in heaven") is absent in some early manuscripts (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus). If original, it asserts the Son's continuing heavenly reality even while incarnate — a high Christological claim. Its presence or absence does not change the main point but affects how one reads the nature of the Incarnation.
14:
ὑψωθῆναι (hypsōthēnai) — the aorist passive infinitive of hypsoō: "to be lifted up." This is the first of three Johannine "lifting up" sayings (cf. John 8:28; John 12:32). The verb does double work: it refers to the physical elevation of crucifixion and simultaneously to exaltation/glorification. The Evangelist does not separate these — in John, the cross is not a defeat requiring a subsequent resurrection vindication; it is itself the moment of glorification.

καθώς...οὕτως (kathōs...houtōs) — "just as...so." The comparison establishes a structural parallel: the bronze serpent lifted up in the wilderness (Numbers 21:8–9) provided physical rescue by looking; the Son lifted up provides eternal life by believing. The καθώς introduces the type; the οὕτως the fulfillment.
15:
ἵνα πᾶς ὁ πιστεύων ἐν αὐτῷ ἔχῃ ζωὴν αἰώνιον — the prepositional phrase ἐν αὐτῷ ("in him") is syntactically ambiguous: it can modify either "everyone believing in him" or "have life in him." Most translations attach it to the participle (believing in him), but the phrasing in John 3:16 — which omits ἐν αὐτῷ — suggests the Evangelist may intend both: belief is directed toward him AND life is located in him.

ζωὴν αἰώνιον (zōēn aiōnion, dispute level 2+3) — "eternal life." Aiōnios (disp=3) derives from aiōn (the age, era); the phrase denotes "the life of the coming age" — qualitative first (the kind of life God has; the life of the new creation), quantitative second (endless). The Evangelist will use this phrase seventeen times; it is never merely longevity.
16:
οὕτως (houtōs) — "in this way," not "so much" or "so greatly." The adverb is of manner, not degree: God loved the world in this manner — namely, by giving the Son. The manner of the love is its measure.

ἠγάπησεν (ēgapēsen, aorist) — the giving of the Son is narrated as a single completed historical act, not an ongoing disposition. God did not merely maintain affection; there was a moment of definitive action.

μονογενῆ (monogenē) — "only-born," from monos (only) and gennaō (to beget). The word carries both uniqueness and relational weight: what is given is irreplaceable, and the giving is costly precisely because the relationship being risked is the closest conceivable.

ἵνα μὴ ἀπόληται — purpose clause with the subjunctive of apollymi: "so that he might not perish." Perishing (apollymi) is the alternative state — not punishment freshly imposed, but the destination of those already on the road of judgment (see John 3:18). The purpose of the giving is rescue from an existing trajectory.
17: οὐ γὰρ ἀπέστειλεν...ἵνα κρίνῃ — "for he did not send...in order to judge." The purpose clause with the subjunctive negates the judicial mission. Krinō (to judge/condemn) and sōzō (to save/rescue) are placed in explicit contrast. The world's condemnation is not new — it is the default state (John 3:18); the mission is to provide a way out of it, not to add to it.
18:
κέκριται (kekritai) — perfect passive: "has been judged and remains judged." The judgment on the unbeliever is not a future verdict but a present state — Johannine realized eschatology. Belief un-condemns (ou krinetai, present: "is not being judged"); unbelief leaves the person in an already-existing condition of condemnation.

ὅτι μὴ πεπίστευκεν — "because he has not believed" (perfect indicative): the present state of un-belief is the grounds for the present state of condemnation. The perfect tense marks a settled posture, not a momentary failure.
19:
αὕτη δέ ἐστιν ἡ κρίσις — "and this is the verdict." Krisis is "verdict/judgment," not merely "crisis." The Evangelist uses the singular feminine article with the neuter noun φῶς (phōs, light): the verdict is the arrival of Light into the world and the human response to it.

ἠγάπησαν...τὸ σκότος — "they loved darkness." The verb is the same (agapaō) used of God's love in John 3:16: humans "loved" darkness rather than light. The use of agapē-love for the wrong object makes the indictment precise — it is not ignorance but an orientation of affection.
20:
ὁ φαῦλα πράσσων — "everyone who practices evil things." Prassō (to practice/do habitually) contrasts with poieō (to do an act). The one who practices evil has a settled pattern, not just an occasional failure. This habitual orientation is what drives the avoidance of light.

ἐλεγχθῇ (elenkhthē) — "might be exposed/convicted." The word can mean both "to expose" (reveal what was hidden) and "to convict" (demonstrate guilt). The one who avoids the light fears both: disclosure of what they have done AND the verdict that follows disclosure.
21:
ὁ δὲ ποιῶν τὴν ἀλήθειαν — "the one doing the truth." "Doing truth" (poiōn tēn alētheian) is a Semitic idiom (Hebrew: ʿāśâ ʾĕmet) meaning to act faithfully, with integrity — not merely asserting propositions but living in alignment with reality. The idiom surfaces in the Dead Sea Scrolls as well as the LXX.

ἵνα φανερωθῇ αὐτοῦ τὰ ἔργα — "that his deeds might be manifested." Phaneroō (to make manifest/visible) is a key Johannine verb for revelation. The one who "does truth" comes to the light not despite their deeds but because they welcome the deeds' disclosure; they have nothing to hide from God's light.
22: A narrative transition. μετὰ ταῦτα (meta tauta) — "after these things" — a loose temporal marker the Evangelist uses frequently. The focus shifts from the Nicodemus dialogue to Jesus's ministry in Judaea alongside John's continuing work.
23: Αἰνών (Ainōn) and Σαλείμ (Saleim) — exact location unknown; "Aenon" likely derives from the Aramaic for "springs." The geographic notice "because there was much water there" confirms that John's baptism required immersion or at least significant water — a detail with implications for the mode of baptism, though the text does not develop it theologically.
24: A parenthetical note: John's ministry is concurrent with Jesus's, not sequentially complete. The Synoptics present John's imprisonment before Jesus's main Galilean ministry (Mark 1:14), which creates a chronological question the Evangelist does not address. The note simply establishes that both are active at the same time.
25: ζητήσεως (zētēseōs) — "question/dispute/inquiry." The term can mean a formal disputation or an argument. It arises περὶ καθαρισμοῦ (peri katharismou) — "concerning purification/cleansing." Some manuscripts read "a Jew" (singular) rather than "Jews" (plural) as the other party. The topic — ritual purification — connects John's baptism to Jewish purity concerns and frames the question that John's disciples bring in John 3:26.
26: μαρτυρεῖς (martyreis) — "you testified" (perfect force in context). John's own testimony about Jesus is now being cited against him: the crowds are going to the one John endorsed. The disciples' framing treats this as a problem; John's response in John 3:27–30 treats it as completion.
27:
οὐ δύναται ἄνθρωπος λαμβάνειν οὐδὲν — "a person cannot receive anything." The statement is universal: every role, gift, and sphere of influence is assigned from heaven. John does not claim he could have more; he asserts that what he has is precisely and completely what he was given.

δεδομένον (dedomenon) — perfect passive participle: "given and remaining given" — the gift from heaven is not provisional but permanent. John cannot exceed the assignment, and he has no desire to.
28: οὐκ εἰμί ἐγὼ ὁ Χριστός — "I am not the Messiah." John uses the strong negation to recall his own earlier testimony (John 1:20). The contrast is sharp: "I am not" (John) vs. the "I am he" of Jesus in John 4:26. John defines himself by negation; Jesus by self-disclosure.
29:
ὁ φίλος τοῦ νυμφίου — "the friend of the bridegroom." In Jewish custom, the shōshbîn was a designated intermediary who arranged the marriage, carried messages between bride and groom, and at the wedding stood with the bridegroom. His role was complete when the bridegroom arrived. John describes his own ministry in precisely these terms: he arranged the meeting; the bridegroom is here.

χαρᾷ χαίρει (charā chairei) — a cognate dative: "rejoices with joy." The construction is emphatic, idiomatic in Greek for the intensity of an emotion. John's joy at Jesus's ascendancy is not resigned but fully realized. πεπλήρωται (peplērōtai, perfect passive) — "has been filled up/completed" — the joy is already full, not anticipated.
30: αὐξάνειν (auxanein, present) / ἐλαττοῦσθαι (elattousthai, present) — both present infinitives of ongoing processes: "he must keep increasing; I must keep decreasing." The present tense captures a continuous trajectory rather than a single moment. The necessity (dei, "must") is the same divine compulsion as in 3:7 and John 4:4.
31:
ὁ ἄνωθεν ἐρχόμενος — "the one coming from above." Now anōthen (the same word as John 3:3) is used explicitly of Jesus's heavenly origin — confirming the Evangelist's intended meaning in the Nicodemus dialogue. The one who comes from above is above all; the one who is from the earth can only speak from within the limits of earth.

ἐπάνω πάντων ἐστίν — "is above all." The phrase appears twice for emphasis. The superiority is ontological, not merely positional.
32:
ὃ ἑώρακεν καὶ ἤκουσεν — "what he has seen and heard." The perfect and aorist combine: ongoing seeing (perfect heōraken) plus completed hearing (aorist ēkousen). The testimony of the one from above is grounded in direct personal knowledge, not report or inference.

καὶ τὴν μαρτυρίαν αὐτοῦ οὐδεὶς λαμβάνει — "and no one receives his testimony." The absolute statement will be qualified in John 3:33 ("whoever has received it"). The "no one" is hyperbolic — the same rhetorical sweep as "all are going to him" in John 3:26 — expressing the scale of rejection, not its totality.
33: ἐσφράγισεν (esphragisen) — "has sealed/certified." To seal something was to authenticate it, confirming its validity by one's own authority. Whoever accepts the testimony of the Son has by that act certified (set their seal to) the truthfulness of God: they have staked their own credibility on God's.
34: οὐ γὰρ ἐκ μέτρου δίδωσιν τὸ πνεῦμα — "for not by measure does he give the Spirit." The subject is unstated and debated. In context, "he" most naturally refers to God giving the Spirit to the Son (cf. John 1:32–33: "I saw the Spirit descending and remaining on him"). The absence of measure signals completeness — the Spirit is not rationed. Contrast the idea that prophets received the Spirit only in measure or for specific tasks.
35: δέδωκεν (dedōken, perfect) — "has given and continues to entrust." The perfect tense marks the Father's delegation as a settled, present reality: the authority is already in the Son's hand and remains there. εἰς τὴν χεῖρα — "into the hand" — a Semitic idiom for authority and stewardship (cf. Psalms 8:6 LXX).
36:
ἀπειθῶν (apeithōn, present participle) — "the one who disobeys/disbelieves." The word combines a- (not) and peithō (to persuade, to comply). Unbelief in John is not merely intellectual non-assent but active refusal, closer to disobedience than mere uncertainty. The present tense marks it as an ongoing posture.

ἡ ὀργὴ τοῦ θεοῦ μένει ἐπ᾽ αὐτόν — "the wrath of God remains on him." Menō (to remain/abide) is a key Johannine word. The wrath is not newly imposed on the unbeliever; it already rests on humanity apart from Christ. It does not arrive; it stays. This is the same structure as the condemnation in John 3:18: the default state is under wrath, and faith moves one out of it — not into wrath.
